Nanoclaw destroys OpenClaw. OpenClaw just got a serious challenger and it's tiny like 3,900 lines of code. Tiny. Nanocclaw is the new open-source AI agent framework that could make OpenClaw's 400,000 lines look completely overkill. We're talking full AI automation, WhatsApp and Telegram connected running on Claude in a fraction of the size. And today I'm breaking down which one actually wins for [music] your business and why the answer might shock you. Now, AI agent frameworks. This sounds super technical, but once you get it, it changes how you think about automating your business forever. We've talked about OpenClaw on this channel before. It's powerful. It's popular. But here's the question nobody's really asking. Do you actually need 400,000 lines of code just to run an AI agent? That's what Nano Claw is asking. And honestly, the answer is no. Stick with me, all right? Because by the end of this video, you're going to know exactly which framework fits your situation. And I've got a real world example showing how I'd use one of these to run the AI profit boardroom that's coming up. Let me keep this dead simple. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Both NanoClaw and OpenClaw are open-source frameworks. So that means anyone can download them, use them, and build on top of them for free. They both connect an AI like Claude to your messaging apps, WhatsApp, Telegram, whatever you already use to talk to people. So instead of manually replying to every message, your AI agent handles it. It can answer questions, take actions, qualify leads, all in real time inside the apps your customers already live in. That's the big picture. Now, here's where these two tools are very, very different. Let's start with the numbers because they tell the whole story. OpenClaw over 434,000 lines of code, 3,680 files. Nanoclaw, around 3,900 lines of code, 15 files. Read that again. 15 files versus 3,600 files. Now, you might think more code means more features. And yeah, sometimes that's true, but more code also means more things that can break, more things you don't understand, more places for security issues to hide, more complexity when you're trying to actually run this thing. Nano claw looked at openclaw and said what if we just built the core of this clean and nothing else. That's the philosophy difference between these two tools and it matters more than most people realize. Now before I go deeper if you're watching this and thinking I want to actually be using AI agents like this to grow my business then the AI profit boardroom is exactly where you need to be need. It's the best community to scale your business, get more customers and save hundreds of hours with AI automation. We go deep on tools like nanoclaw and openclaw. Not just the theory, the actual setup, the real workflows, the stuff that moves the needle. Link is in the comment and description. Go check it out. Okay, back to it because we need to talk about how Nanoclaw actually works under the hood. And this is where it gets interesting. Nanoclaw runs as a single Node.js process. That's it. It connects to WhatsApp or Telegram, picks up messages, sends them to Claude, delivers the response back. But here's the smart part. It uses containers, specifically Docker or Apple container. What that means is every group conversation gets its own isolated container. So if something goes wrong in one group, a bad prompt, a weird input, anything. It doesn't affect anything else. That's OS level isolation. The operating system itself puts a hard wall around each conversation. And that's a bigger deal than it sounds. Imagine you're running the AI profit boardroom on Telegram. You've got multiple group chats, members, leads, coaching calls. With Nanoclaw, each of those groups runs in its own container, totally separated. If something breaks in one, the rest keep running fine. That's real security, not just a checkbox. Actual hard walls. Open Claw is different. It runs at the app level. It's got way more features, broader model support. It works with way more AI providers, not just Claude. It's got a permissions dashboard so you can control who has access to what. It's been getting updates all through early 2026. model support improvements, UI improvements, more integrations, but the security checks happen inside the app itself. Not at the OS level. If a vulnerability exists in the app code, the app is your only line of defense. With NanoClaw, you've got the container wall, even if something goes sideways in the code. That's a different risk profile, and for most people, Nanoclaw's approach is the safer one. Here's something most people in this space don't think about until it's too late. When you're running an AI agent that's talking to your customers or your community members, you need to be able to audit it. You need to know what is it actually doing? What data is it touching? What is it running? Right? With OpenClaw, 400,000 lines of code, nearly 4,000 files. That is a serious audit. You'd need a full engineering team just to understand what's happening inside it. With NanoClaw, 15 files under 4,000 lines. You can read the entire codebase yourself in an afternoon. genuinely. That's called auditability. And for any business owner deploying AI that touches customers, auditability is not optional. It's critical. Think about it. If your AI profit boardroom community assistant goes off script, starts giving wrong advice, sharing bad information, doing something unexpected, you need to find that in the code fast. With Nanoclaw, you can. With OpenClaw, that's a much harder problem. Let me show you exactly how I'd set this up in the real world. Say we want a Nanoclaw agent running inside our AI profit boardroom Telegram group. Here's how it works. First, you connect Nanoclaw to the Telegram bot. That's your entry point. Second, you define the agent's job. Something like this. You are an AI assistant for the AI profit boardroom, a community for business owners who want to grow using AI automation. When a member asks a question, give them a clear, practical answer about AI tools, workflows, or strategy. If they ask about resources, point them to the right community channel. If they want more support, let them know they can message the team directly. Third, you configure the containers. Each group chat gets its own isolated environment. The main community group, the coaching group, the leads group, all separate, all protected. Fourth, you use Nanoclaw's built-in skills. There's one called slashupdate NanoClaw. It lets you safely pull in upstream code changes without breaking anything. So when Nanoclaw ships updates, which they do, you can update safely. No manual patching, no hoping you don't break production. The whole thing runs lean. One process, 15 files, you know exactly what it's doing because you can read every single line. That's the kind of AI automation that actually works in a real business. Now, OpenClaw isn't useless. Not even close. If you're a developer, if you're building a product on top of an AI agent framework, if you need to support multiple AI models, not just Claude, OpenClaw is the more powerful choice. March 2026 saw OpenClaw drop new releases covering expanded model support and a permissions dashboard. That's real functionality that NanoClaw doesn't have yet. OpenClaw is also way more model agnostic. You know, you're not locked into Claude. You can run whatever model fits your use case. So if you're building something complex, a multi-tenant tool, something that needs multiple model providers, OpenClaw has the infrastructure for that. But if you're an individual operator, a business owner running AI agents for your own community or clients, NanoClaw is almost certainly the smarter call. Let me break it down clean. Use Nanoclaw if you want something simple, auditable, and secure. If you're running AI agents for yourself or a small team, if you care about being able to understand and verify exactly what your AI is doing, if you want fast deployment with minimal complexity, and if you're fine with claude as your model, use OpenClaw if you need support for multiple AI models, if you're building something more complex or at larger scale, if you have developer resources to manage a bigger code base, and if you need a permissions dashboard and more enterprise style controls. The honest truth is most people watching this video nano claw is the right starting point. You can always graduate to openclaw later. But starting with 400,000 lines of code when 15 files does the job, that's not progress. That's complexity for its own sake. One thing both these tools share and this matters a lot. They're both open source. No vendor locking. No monthly fee to the framework. No company that can pull the rug on you. You run it. You own it. You control it. In a world where AI tools are changing every few weeks, that kind of control is worth a lot. Think about how fast things have moved just from late 2025 into early 2026. New models, new frameworks, new tools every single week. With open-source, you're not waiting for a vendor to update their product. You are in control. Here's the thing about Nanoclaw. It's early. It's minimal by design, but minimal doesn't mean limited. It means focused. As the AI agent space keeps maturing through 2026, the question isn't just which framework has the most features is which framework you can actually trust, audit, and deploy without a team of engineers. Nanoclaw is betting on simplicity winning. And looking at the history of tech from Linux to Skeite, the small auditable tool almost always outlasts the massive complex one. Openclaw has the feature set. Nanoclaw has the clarity and right now for most business owners automating with AI clarity wins.
